Insider Trading.title: Theft, not fairness
Insider Trading.plain: The law doesn't ban trading on an informational edge — it bans stealing information that belongs to someone else.
Insider Trading.quote: “Insider trading,” I like to say, “is not about fairness, it’s about theft.” Lots of people trade securities with an information advantage ; you are very much allowed to trade while knowing things that other people don’t know.
Insider Trading.quote_title: You Can Insider Trade NFTs Now
Insider Trading.analysis: Matt returns to this distinction constantly because prosecutors keep getting it wrong. Every time the SEC brings a case, they invoke fairness — 'level playing fields,' 'cheaters who got the answers before the test.' Matt finds this maddening because it's not what the law actually says, and it produces confused outcomes. The real test is simpler: did you steal information that belongs to someone else?

Insider Trading.title: The tipping chain
Insider Trading.plain: Liability flows down through chains of people — the insider, the tipper, the tippee, and beyond.
Insider Trading.quote: The outsider (the “tippee”) traded on inside information that he got nefariously, and you (the “tipper”) gave him that information with nefarious intent. You got a “personal benefit,” in the language of the court cases, and so you are equally in trouble.
Insider Trading.analysis: The chain can extend surprisingly far — the insider tells a friend, who tells a spouse, who tells a broker. At each link, liability can attach, provided the person knew or should have known the information was confidential. This structure means that being three degrees removed from the source doesn't protect you if you understood you were receiving privileged material.

Insider Trading.title: Personal benefit
Insider Trading.plain: To convict a tipper, prosecutors must prove they got something in return — even just psychological satisfaction.
Insider Trading.quote: To convict a tipper, prosecutors must prove they got something in return — even just psychological satisfaction. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Insider Trading.analysis: The requirement sounds strange but has a real purpose — it screens out cases where someone casually mentions a deal without any intent to enable trading. The problem is that courts have interpreted 'personal benefit' so broadly that almost any social exchange qualifies: a friendship, a shared dinner, the psychological pleasure of being important. Matt reads this as the legal system trying to capture the true wrong while struggling to articulate it precisely.

Insider Trading.title: Your own intentions
Insider Trading.plain: Trading on information you generated yourself — your own plans and decisions — is always legal.
Insider Trading.quote: You’re trading on your own intentions. That strikes me as the legal sort of commodity insider trading, though I should emphasize that we are in extremely uncharted waters here and I really have no idea.
Insider Trading.analysis: This is one of Matt's cleaner pronouncements: you can't steal from yourself. If the information originates with you — your plans, your intentions, your decisions — you're not misappropriating anything. Carl Icahn buying a stock before announcing a stake isn't insider trading. It's just good timing on your own idea. The complications come when your 'intentions' belong partly to your employer.

Insider Trading.title: The 15 Laws
Insider Trading.plain: Matt's running catalogue of how insider traders get caught, formulated from years of SEC enforcement.
Insider Trading.quote: Of course if he had told her she might well have told him to knock it off, so really this is just a version of the First Law, which is: Don’t insider trade. (Also the Second Law: And not with the call options).
Insider Trading.quote_title: Insider Trading Is Not a Romantic Surprise
Insider Trading.analysis: What started as a running joke became one of the most useful checklists in financial journalism. Each law documents a genuine failure mode — real cases, real crimes, real reasons people got caught. The meta-lesson is that insider trading is almost always undone by predictable human errors: digital trails, third-party witnesses, patterns that screamed to any statistical analysis.

Insider Trading.title: Prediction markets
Insider Trading.plain: A new frontier where the same logic applies but the rules haven't fully caught up.
Insider Trading.quote: Other sports sometimes have stricter rules about insider betting, but not always. And people who set up prediction markets are more likely to argue that insider trading is good.
Insider Trading.quote_title: Insider Betting Can Make Things Weird
Insider Trading.analysis: This is the edge of the map for insider trading law. The core logic applies — you have material nonpublic information, you trade on it, someone loses. But prediction market operators aren't registered exchanges, the 'securities' involved aren't traditional, and prosecutors haven't tested these cases aggressively yet. Matt watches this space with particular interest because it forces clarity about what the law actually protects.

Options / Derivatives.title: Everything is secretly an option
Options / Derivatives.plain: Limit orders, executive pay, corporate bonds — once you see them as options, you can't unsee it.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Speed bumps The paradox at the heart of market structure is that “Standing Limit Orders Are Trading Options That Offer Liquidity.”[1] If a stock is trading at $100, and I place an order to buy it at $99.
Options / Derivatives.analysis: This is one of Matt's most generative conceptual moves. The moment you see a limit order as an unpriced put option, market-making becomes a story about option risk rather than just bid-ask spreads. Executive pay becomes a story about asymmetric payoffs. Corporate bonds become a story about who holds the option to default. Finance looks completely different once you've put on these glasses.

Options / Derivatives.title: The Second Law
Options / Derivatives.plain: Don't buy short-dated out-of-the-money call options on merger targets. That's literally the first thing regulators check.
Options / Derivatives.quote: You can trade any instruments you want—buy short-dated out-of-the-money options, whatever—and don’t worry (for now!) about the legal issues.
Options / Derivatives.analysis: The reason this is Law #2 (right after 'don't do it') is that it's simultaneously the most profitable way to insider trade and the most obvious signal to regulators. Short-dated out-of-the-money calls have enormous leverage — small investments, huge payoffs if the stock moves sharply. But that exact profile, in the days before a merger announcement, lights up every statistical screen the SEC runs. The Second Law is also a good trade for innocent reasons, which is why it keeps appearing in the archive.

Options / Derivatives.title: CDS and engineered defaults
Options / Derivatives.plain: Credit default swaps turned lawyers into traders — you profit when a company defaults, so some people started manufacturing defaults.
Options / Derivatives.quote: (There are variations, like paying the company extra to issue new bonds that will increase the CDS payoff. You can do that part without a manufactured default, or even without a default at all.
Options / Derivatives.analysis: Matt has covered this story across multiple years with fascination that never quite tips into alarm. The instrument is neutral — insurance against corporate default is genuinely useful. But once you realize that 'insurance' also creates an incentive to cause the insured event, you see why credit derivatives require their own regulatory ecosystem. The Windstream case was particularly elegant: a hedge fund used legitimate legal process to manufacture a technical default on a financially healthy company.

Options / Derivatives.title: Options in executive pay
Options / Derivatives.plain: Stock options as compensation are just call options on the company — the executive wins big if the stock rises, risks nothing if it falls.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Someone will pay you today for that uncertain future value—that’s how stock options work—so you can collect some cash.[5] This is all quite well known to hedge funds.
Options / Derivatives.analysis: The shift to stock options in the 1990s was supposed to align CEO incentives with shareholders. It mostly did — and also created a new set of misalignments. Options only pay if the stock goes up, which means CEOs are incentivized toward volatility and risk-taking. The option has no downside for the executive. This asymmetry shows up everywhere in Matt's coverage of corporate governance.

Options / Derivatives.title: Retail options and meme stocks
Options / Derivatives.plain: When retail traders buy calls en masse, market makers must buy the underlying stock to hedge, pushing prices up — a feedback loop.
Options / Derivatives.quote: [2] If I am a market maker, standing ready to buy or sell the stock, it’s even worse: When the stock is at $100, not only do I put in a bid to buy it at $99.99, I also put in an offer to sell it at $100.
Options / Derivatives.analysis: The GameStop saga revealed a feedback loop that most market observers hadn't fully modeled. When a stock is heavily shorted and retail traders pile into call options, the mechanics of dealer hedging can create a self-reinforcing squeeze. Matt covered this not as a scandal but as a genuinely interesting market structure story — a case where small retail actors accidentally discovered how to exploit institutional dynamics.

Options / Derivatives.title: The volatility trade
Options / Derivatives.plain: Volatility itself can be bought and sold. Traders don't just bet on where a stock goes — they bet on how much it moves.
Options / Derivatives.quote: Volatility is up. There is an overhang of several very large rumored unicorn initial public offerings, most notably one from Uber Technologies Inc.
Options / Derivatives.quote_title: Direct Listings Are a Thing Now
Options / Derivatives.analysis: Volatility is the one asset class that's genuinely different from all others: you can't hold it directly, it mean-reverts reliably, and it explodes during crises at the exact moment everything else does too. Matt covers the volatility space as a window into how sophisticated traders think about uncertainty itself — not just whether something will happen, but how much disagreement there is about whether it will happen.

Securities Fraud.title: Everything is securities fraud
Securities Fraud.plain: Matt's central shtick: almost anything a public company does wrong can be reframed as securities fraud by shareholders.
Securities Fraud.quote: Everything is securities fraud Is it securities fraud for a public company to have only one Black member of its board of directors? We talked last week about a shareholder lawsuit against Oracle Corp.
Securities Fraud.quote_title: They’re All Friends in the Index
Securities Fraud.analysis: The bit works because it's largely accurate. Public companies must disclose material information to investors, and the definition of 'material' expands to cover almost anything that moves the stock price. A CEO's undisclosed health crisis? Material. A secret environmental liability? Material. Systemic sexual harassment the board knew about? Also material, and the executives who sold stock while knowing about it may have committed insider trading on top of the fraud. The joke and the legal reality converge.

Securities Fraud.title: The disclosure machine
Securities Fraud.plain: Companies must disclose material information. The question is always: what counts as material, and when?
Securities Fraud.quote: (I think that is kind of the point of public-company disclosure requirements?) Certainly they are taking more risk by buying illiquid pre-IPO shares than they would by buying in, or after, the IPO.
Securities Fraud.quote_title: Mutual Funds in the Enchanted Forest
Securities Fraud.analysis: Materiality is a legal standard — 'a substantial likelihood that a reasonable investor would consider the information important.' Courts apply it case by case. Matt's contribution is to show how elastic it is in practice: companies want to disclose as little as possible, plaintiffs want everything to be material, and the line between them is contested in nearly every securities lawsuit.

Securities Fraud.title: 10b5-1 plans
Securities Fraud.plain: Executives set up pre-scheduled selling plans to trade legally even when they know things. Except the plans can be gamed.
Securities Fraud.quote: Executives set up pre-scheduled selling plans to trade legally even when they know things. Except the plans can be gamed. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Securities Fraud.analysis: The plans were designed to solve a real problem: executives have material information constantly, so when are they ever allowed to trade? The solution was to set up automatic selling schedules when they don't have inside information — in theory. In practice, executives set up plans, acquire new material information, and the automatic sales continue. The SEC has tightened the rules, but the fundamental tension between 'I run this company' and 'I need to sell stock' persists.

Securities Fraud.title: The class action machine
Securities Fraud.plain: When a stock drops on bad news, a class action follows within days. It's almost automatic.
Securities Fraud.quote: I find that roughly 16% of securities class actions arise from conduct where the most direct victims are not shareholders. However,
Securities Fraud.analysis: The pattern is almost mechanical: company stock drops more than 10% on bad news, plaintiff's law firm files within days, case proceeds to discovery. Matt covers these with a mixture of amusement and genuine respect for the underlying logic — shareholders who bought stock on misleading statements have a real grievance, even if the litigation process has become largely transactional.

Securities Fraud.title: Pump and dump
Securities Fraud.plain: Say something false to move a stock, then sell into the pop. Ancient crime, infinite modern variations.
Securities Fraud.quote: We talk about pump-and-dump schemes a lot around here, in which scammers buy stocks, put out fake but bullish reports and then dump the stocks. How do their reports move the stocks?
Securities Fraud.quote_title: Andrew Left Wasn’t Short for Long
Securities Fraud.analysis: The scheme is ancient but keeps finding new media. Stock message boards, then crypto Telegram channels, then TikTok. The underlying mechanics never change: accumulate quietly, hype loudly, sell into the noise. What changes is the scale and the demographics of the victims, and whether the SEC's enforcement bandwidth can keep up with the innovation pace.

Securities Fraud.title: CEO misconduct and stock sales
Securities Fraud.plain: If a CEO is hiding something material and sells stock, that sale itself becomes evidence of insider trading.
Securities Fraud.quote: If a CEO is hiding something material and sells stock, that sale itself becomes evidence of insider trading. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Securities Fraud.analysis: The CBS/Moonves case crystallized this argument for Matt. If a CEO is hiding something material — even something that predates any conscious intention to hide it — and then sells stock, every one of those sales potentially constitutes insider trading. The 10b5-1 plan defense fails if it was established while the CEO already knew the hidden information. This means the cover-up and the crime become inseparable.

M&A / Mergers.title: How deals get done
M&A / Mergers.plain: Investment banks run a structured process — the target, the bidder, the board, the fairness opinion, the deal premium.
M&A / Mergers.quote: ) Instead, the trading price of the stock before the deal was announced—$17.13 per share—was the best evidence of the fair price, so that’s what he gave the plaintiffs.
M&A / Mergers.analysis: The merger process has a formal choreography that Matt has explained in detail across hundreds of issues. Board approves exploration, banker runs process, potential buyers sign NDAs and get access to data rooms, bids come in, negotiation over price and terms, definitive agreement signed, shareholders vote, regulators weigh in, deal closes or doesn't. Each step has its own legal structure and its own failure modes.

M&A / Mergers.title: Material adverse change
M&A / Mergers.plain: The clause that lets buyers walk away from signed deals — and the litigation that follows when they try.
M&A / Mergers.quote: With no financing contingency or diligence condition, the agreement gave Musk no out absent a Company Material Adverse Effect or a material covenant breach by Twitter. Musk had to try to conjure one of those.
M&A / Mergers.analysis: The MAC clause exists to let buyers escape deals when something fundamental about the target changes before closing. Courts interpret it very narrowly — routine business downturns don't qualify, even bad ones. The Delaware courts spent years developing doctrine that essentially tells buyers: if you signed the deal, you're probably stuck with it. COVID tested this framework across dozens of deals simultaneously.

M&A / Mergers.title: Activist shareholders
M&A / Mergers.plain: Hedge funds buy stakes and agitate for deals, spinoffs, or management changes — sometimes helpfully, sometimes destructively.
M&A / Mergers.quote: ” Carl Icahn didn’t like it, and sued, and is talking about a proxy fight. But when you read the “Background of the Merger,” you get a more sympathetic picture of this structure.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: You Can’t Vote on a Deal With No Deal
M&A / Mergers.analysis: Matt covers activists with genuine ambivalence. They often have a point — poorly run companies do trade at discounts, and sometimes a proxy fight is the only mechanism that forces accountability. But activism is also a financial strategy that sometimes prioritizes short-term stock moves over long-term business health. The same activist can be right about the diagnosis and wrong about the cure.

M&A / Mergers.title: Deal litigation
M&A / Mergers.plain: Almost every large acquisition triggers shareholder lawsuits. Delaware courts decide most of them.
M&A / Mergers.quote: Vintage, meanwhile, sued in Delaware Chancery Court. There was not much to say, but Vintage said it.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Don’t Forget to Do Your Merger
M&A / Mergers.analysis: Delaware's Court of Chancery has effectively become the Supreme Court of American corporate law. Vice chancellors issue opinions that shape how every large deal is structured. Matt reads these decisions carefully and often explains them with evident pleasure — the legal reasoning is sophisticated, the stakes are enormous, and the outcomes shape corporate governance for years.

M&A / Mergers.title: Going private
M&A / Mergers.plain: Taking a public company private removes scrutiny and unlocks flexibility — if you can get shareholders to sell.
M&A / Mergers.quote: Would it have been able to go back to its private backers for $13 billion of capital after doing the largest leveraged buyout in history? Maybe, I don’t know; private markets were pretty generous for much of that time.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Elon Musk Had a Good Day
M&A / Mergers.analysis: The going-private narrative usually involves a stock that management thinks is undervalued, a founder who wants to escape quarterly earnings pressure, or a private equity firm that thinks it can cut costs faster than the public markets will allow. Matt has covered dozens of these transactions and notes that the same arguments recur: public company life is burdensome, private companies can take longer-term views, and somehow investors always seem to get bought out right before the business improves.

M&A / Mergers.title: Antitrust
M&A / Mergers.plain: Regulators can block deals they think harm competition. The standard keeps shifting with political winds.
M&A / Mergers.quote: They also agreed that, if the deal was terminated on the drop-dead date because they hadn’t gotten antitrust approval, Vintage would pay Rent-A-Center a $126.5 million breakup fee.
M&A / Mergers.quote_title: Don’t Forget to Do Your Merger
M&A / Mergers.analysis: Merger antitrust review became dramatically more aggressive in the Biden era and has recalibrated under Trump. The same transaction that sailed through in 2018 would face an enforcement action in 2023. Matt covers the doctrinal shifts as genuine intellectual history — changing theories about how to measure competitive harm, when vertical integration matters, and whether market power in adjacent industries should trigger scrutiny.

Bankruptcy.title: Chapter 11 basics
Bankruptcy.plain: Companies can restructure debts in bankruptcy while continuing to operate — a breathing space creditors don't get.
Bankruptcy.quote: In the case of Voyager, it filed for bankruptcy, and FTX offered to come in, move Voyager’s customers to FTX, and cash out anyone who wanted out. In either case the basic point was that FTX had enough money to cash out everyone, so no one needed to cash out .
Bankruptcy.analysis: The insight that makes Chapter 11 remarkable is that it inverts the normal relationship between a company and its obligations. Outside bankruptcy, the company owes its creditors and must pay. Inside bankruptcy, the company becomes a 'debtor in possession' that can run itself while a court supervises the restructuring. Creditors get to vote on a reorganization plan, but the company keeps operating — it doesn't have to be liquidated just because it can't pay its bills.

Bankruptcy.title: Creditor hierarchy
Bankruptcy.plain: Who gets paid first? Secured lenders, then unsecured bondholders, then equity — usually in that order, but lawyers spend years fighting over the details.
Bankruptcy.quote: At this point, the normal outcome is that you file for bankruptcy and your creditors get your assets, which are worth less than the amount they were owed. But all these things are uncertain and decisions need to be made fast, so mistakes are made.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: FTX Is Still Looking for Money
Bankruptcy.analysis: The absolute priority rule is the bedrock of bankruptcy finance: secured creditors get paid first (from the assets securing their loans), then unsecured creditors in order of seniority, then equity — which usually gets nothing in a real bankruptcy. This hierarchy creates fierce litigation because the value of a reorganized company is finite, and each dollar that goes to senior creditors is a dollar that doesn't reach junior ones.

Bankruptcy.title: FTX and crypto collapse
Bankruptcy.plain: SBF's exchange mixed customer funds with trading operations, creating a $8B hole. Matt saw the structure coming.
Bankruptcy.quote: On Thursday, FTX’s bankruptcy lawyers sued Mr. Kives’s firm, K5 Global, to recover the money that Mr.
Bankruptcy.analysis: Matt had been writing skeptically about FTX for months before the collapse, and his post-collapse coverage was remarkable for its accuracy about the mechanics. The company wasn't just insolvent — it had been secretly using customer deposits as operating capital for its trading arm, Alameda. The accounting was so bad that nobody, including insiders, could say where the money was. Matt's coverage treated this as a failure of basic financial infrastructure, not just fraud.

Bankruptcy.title: WeWork's spectacular fall
Bankruptcy.plain: From $47 billion to bankruptcy in four years — the story of how narrative can substitute for economics, until it can't.
Bankruptcy.quote: Cole Capital We talked a couple of weeks ago about a fake press release from a fake company called “Cole Capital Funds” pretending it would buy WeWork Inc. for $9 per share.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: Fake WeWork Rescuer Was an Hour Late
Bankruptcy.analysis: The WeWork story is about the collision of Silicon Valley growth-at-all-costs thinking with real estate economics. Real estate leases are long-term, fixed obligations; WeWork's revenue was short-term and cyclical. Adam Neumann sold investors on 'space as a service' and 'community-adjusted EBITDA,' a metric he invented to make the losses look better. Matt covered the IPO attempt with barely concealed amusement and the eventual bankruptcy without surprise.

Bankruptcy.title: Liability management
Bankruptcy.plain: Companies use complex debt exchanges to avoid bankruptcy — moving assets away from creditors in ways that are technically legal but deeply contentious.
Bankruptcy.quote: The two current trustees are both lawyers (as you’d expect of trustees), not oilmen or financial experts (as you might expect of oil-company directors).[4] There are no annual shareholder meetings, and there have been only four shareholder meetings in the last 30 years.
Bankruptcy.analysis: The past decade produced a wave of financial engineering in the distressed credit world that courts are still working through. Companies facing debt maturities would use covenant loopholes to move valuable assets away from existing lenders — 'dropping down' IP, transferring subsidiaries, doing debt exchanges that left some lenders behind. Matt covers these transactions in careful technical detail because they reveal how creditor rights actually work in practice.

Bankruptcy.title: Distressed investing
Bankruptcy.plain: Buying the debt of struggling companies and profiting from reorganization — sometimes by steering the outcome.
Bankruptcy.quote: Much later, in 2017, a hedge fund called Aurelius Capital Management LP acquired some of those Windstream bonds and sued, claiming that Windstream had violated its bond indenture and was in default. Windstream fought back hard, but eventually Aurelius won.
Bankruptcy.quote_title: Maybe Companies Will Get Rid of CDS
Bankruptcy.analysis: Buying debt at a discount and profiting from reorganization is a legitimate financial strategy — and also a form of activism that can accelerate or shape corporate collapses. Matt covers the ethical ambiguity here without resolving it. A hedge fund that buys CDS on a company and then engineers a default is doing something technically legal but economically predatory. The same fund might also be the only one willing to provide liquidity in a genuine crisis.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: The basic innovation
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Crypto created a mechanism to produce artificial scarcity digitally — and built an entire financial system on top of it.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: Crypto created a mechanism to produce artificial scarcity digitally — and built an entire financial system on top of it. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: Matt's position on crypto has always been: the technology is genuinely interesting, the financial applications are real, the valuations are often crazy. Bitcoin solved a hard computer science problem — how to create digital scarcity without a central authority. That's worth something. How much is worth something is a separate question that the market has answered in wildly varying ways across a decade.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Is it a security?
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: The SEC's central question for a decade: does a crypto token count as a security? The answer determines who regulates it.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: [2] And so the SEC decided that most actual ICO tokens are securities: They are used to raise money from investors with a promise of financial returns from a common enterprise, which is enough (under the “Howey test” of U.S.
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: The Howey test — an investment of money in a common enterprise with an expectation of profits from others' efforts — was written in 1946 to cover orange grove investments. Applying it to tokens requires analogy and interpretation. Matt has followed the debate across hundreds of cases and finds the legal uncertainty genuinely frustrating: the SEC's position that most tokens are unregistered securities means the entire industry has been operating in a legal gray zone for a decade.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Stablecoins
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Dollar-pegged tokens that promise $1 in, $1 out. The promise is harder to keep than it sounds.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: That cryptocurrency is a “stablecoin,” meaning that one Tether is supposed to be worth exactly one U.S.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Things Got Weird for Stablecoin Tether
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: The promise seems simple: a dollar-pegged token that's always redeemable for one dollar. The reality depends entirely on what backs it — actual dollars in a bank, dollar-denominated assets, or an algorithmic mechanism that promises to maintain the peg through financial engineering. Terra/Luna showed what happens when the algorithm fails under stress: a bank run that wiped out $40 billion in a week. Matt covered it as an almost classical monetary economics story.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: DeFi and smart contracts
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Finance without intermediaries — code executes automatically. But code has bugs, and bugs mean hacks.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: 00 per share to PSTH shareholders (“SPARs”) which can only be exercised after SPARC enters into a definitive agreement for its initial business combination. The SPARs are expected to trade on the NYSE and have a term of five years, subject to extension….
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Bill Ackman’s SPAC Will Be Three SPACs
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: The innovation in DeFi is real: you can create lending, trading, and derivatives protocols that operate automatically, transparently, and without intermediaries. The limitation is equally real: smart contracts can't know things that aren't on the blockchain, can't adapt to circumstances their authors didn't anticipate, and can't be easily fixed when someone finds a bug. Matt has covered dozens of DeFi exploits and treats each one as a lesson in what 'code is law' actually means when the code is wrong.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: The pivot to blockchain
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: In 2017, tiny companies could boost their stock price 300% just by saying 'blockchain.' The SEC noticed.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: In fact, excluding secondary market transactions, as of today, Kin exceeds Ether and Bitcoin (which Director Hinman does not believe are securities) in daily blockchain activity, demonstrating Kin’s wide acceptance and adoption. (See https://blocktivity.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote_title: Kik Gets in Trouble Over Tokens
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: This episode is one of Matt's favorites as pure financial absurdity. In late 2017, you could triple a company's stock price in a day by announcing a pivot to blockchain — even if you had no plan, no blockchain expertise, and no coherent explanation of how blockchain related to your business. The Long Island Iced Tea Corporation renaming itself Long Blockchain Corp is the emblematic case. The SEC eventually brought cases against many of these companies.

Crypto / Blockchain.title: Bitcoin treasury companies
Crypto / Blockchain.plain: Companies that raise money to buy Bitcoin — a new financial vehicle Matt has watched with fascinated skepticism.
Crypto / Blockchain.quote: MicroStrategy Inc. (now doing business as Strategy), the great financial innovator of our age, has delightfully solved both problems in the simplest possible way.
Crypto / Blockchain.analysis: MicroStrategy invented a new financial model: raise debt, buy Bitcoin, watch your stock price track Bitcoin's volatility with extra leverage. The model has spread, been refined, and now has imitators across multiple countries. Matt covers it with fascination — it's simultaneously an interesting financial engineering trick and a complete abandonment of any pretense that the company's core business matters.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: Funding secured
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's August 2018 tweet that he was taking Tesla private at $420 'funding secured' cost him $20M and the SEC chairmanship.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: In August 2018, he tweeted “considering taking Tesla private at $420. Funding secured,” and spent a few days pretending it might happen.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: Elon Musk Had a Good Day
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: The tweet is the most consequential CEO communication failure in modern financial history. Eight words — 'Am considering taking Tesla private at $420. Funding secured.' — triggered an SEC investigation, a $20M personal fine, and years of legal consequences. The 'funding' wasn't secured. The '420' was a cannabis joke aimed at his girlfriend. He composed it without consulting lawyers. Matt has written about this episode as a study in what happens when a CEO with enormous market power is also constitutionally unable to filter his communications.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: The Twitter acquisition
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk agreed to buy Twitter for $54.20/share, tried to walk away, got sued, and ended up owning it anyway.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: ” All of these problems mean that, if you are a Twitter shareholder and Musk says “I am going to buy Twitter for $54.20 per share,” you might not believe him. “Show me the money,” you might reasonably say.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: Musk agreed to buy Twitter for $54.20/share, appeared to develop buyer's remorse, cited bot counts as a reason to walk away, got sued by Twitter's board, and ultimately closed the deal. Matt covered each stage with analytical precision: the original agreement, the material adverse change arguments, Delaware's unwillingness to let buyers escape signed deals, and the eventual $44B acquisition of a company that immediately began losing advertisers.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: Musk's pay packages
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Tesla's CEO pay votes keep getting struck down by courts, approved by shareholders, struck down again — a loop.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: 20, and then he refused to pay and had no good reason for backing out of the deal, and the seller had to turn around and sell the thing to someone else for $25 instead, then the seller could go to court and demand that Musk pay the $29.20 difference.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: The Price of Not Buying Twitter
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: Tesla's board approved a 2018 pay package worth potentially $55 billion. A Delaware court struck it down in 2024 as the product of a conflicted process — the board members approving the pay were close friends of Musk who weren't adequately independent. Shareholders then voted to reinstate the package. Delaware then struck it down again. The loop reveals fundamental tensions in how public company CEO pay is governed when the CEO is also the company's dominant cultural force.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: SpaceX and X.AI
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's private companies create constant related-party transaction headaches for Tesla's public shareholders.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: He once tweeted that the new Tesla Roadster would, um, fly: “Will use SpaceX cold gas thruster system with ultra high pressure air in a composite over-wrapped pressure vessel in place of the 2 rear seats.” I am genuinely uncertain how seriously that was meant.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote_title: Don’t Take Elon Musk So Seriously
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: Musk runs several large private companies while running Tesla, a large public company. The potential conflicts are obvious: personnel, technology, contracts, even attention. Matt covers the related-party transaction disclosures carefully. Each time Tesla enters a deal with SpaceX — for satellite connectivity, for manufacturing, for anything — the question is whether Tesla shareholders got a fair deal or whether Musk effectively transferred value to himself.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: The Elon Markets Hypothesis
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Matt's formulation: markets now price the probability that Musk will do something irrational, because he often does.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: [4] So Tesla’s banks owned warrants to buy Tesla stock. One of those banks was JPMorgan Chase & Co.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: Matt coined this in the context of Tesla's stock trading at multiples that couldn't be justified by traditional valuation. His formulation: markets are pricing in the Musk premium — the probability that this specific person will do something unexpected and enormous. It's a rational response to the evidence. But it also means Tesla's stock is partly a derivative on Musk's mood, attention, and continued involvement — none of which are disclosed in any 10-K.

Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.title: DOGE and government
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.plain: Musk's government efficiency role creates novel securities law questions nobody has asked before.
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.quote: You can’t negotiate a deal and then show up at closing and Elon Musk turns out his pockets and is like “well I have like 69 bucks plus some Dogecoin.” You need to know if he can get the money!
Musk / Tesla / SpaceX.analysis: The Department of Government Efficiency created novel legal territory that Matt found genuinely interesting. A private citizen with no formal government role was accessing government systems, influencing agency budgets, and effectively setting policy — while running public companies that had regulatory relationships with those same agencies. The securities law questions were largely unexplored: what does material nonpublic information mean when the person generating it is both a government actor and a public company CEO?

Hedge Funds / PE.title: The 2-and-20 model
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: 2% annual management fee plus 20% of profits. Sounds reasonable until you do the math on a $10B fund in a flat year.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: 5% annual management fee on their assets, plus 20% of their gains. [3] In 2014, Ackman launched a new fund, Pershing Square Holdings (PSH).
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: The math works beautifully for managers and poorly for investors in most scenarios. A 2% management fee on a $10B fund generates $200M annually regardless of performance. The 20% carry only kicks in above a hurdle rate, but in good years it generates enormous windfalls. Matt has noted that hedge fund fees transferred hundreds of billions from institutional investors (pension funds, endowments) to fund managers over two decades, often for performance that didn't justify the cost.

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Private equity value creation
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: PE firms buy companies with debt, improve them (or just cut costs), then sell. The debate is which part actually creates value.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: Apollo If you are a private equity fund, you borrow a lot of money to buy a company, and then you try to operate the company in a way that makes enough money to pay back the debt and make you rich. Sometimes this works and everyone is happy.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Private Equity Looks Out for Itself
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: The academic literature is genuinely mixed on whether PE creates value or extracts it. There are plausible mechanisms for both. PE firms can professionalize management, focus companies on efficiency, and take longer-term views than public markets allow. They can also load companies with debt that serves their returns while stressing the business, cut jobs to improve near-term cash flow, and exit before the long-term consequences materialize. Matt covers both the successes and the failures without a strong prior.

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Private credit
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Banks pulled back from lending, so private funds stepped in. Now they're so big they're starting to look like banks.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: You probably invest in other stuff — “credit,” corporate bonds and private credit and asset-backed securities — to earn a bit more yield. Much of this credit stuff, though, has shorter terms than 30 years; there is not that much 30-year corporate borrowing.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: People Are Worried About the Basis Trade
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: The private credit boom is one of the major financial structural shifts of the 2020s. Banks, constrained by post-2008 capital requirements, pulled back from corporate lending. Private credit funds — patient capital with long lockups — stepped in. They now provide billions in loans that would previously have come from banks, at higher rates and with more flexibility. Matt has written about this transformation at length: it looks like risk redistribution, but from whom to whom, and at what systemic cost, remains contested.

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Short sellers
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Betting a stock will fall — a profession that's highly regulated, often vilified, and sometimes very right.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: This creates a new set of investors who may be keen to sell -- or hedge their positions by short selling -- when the IPO finally happens. Short-selling of Lyft shares was particularly heavy after its debut.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Lyft and Uber’s Shareholders Were Strangers
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: Matt has unusual sympathy for short sellers, who he views as performing a valuable information-aggregation function that makes markets more accurate. Short sellers do the work of finding overvalued or fraudulent companies and betting against them. They're often right. They're also vilified by company management, occasionally targeted by coordinated retail squeezes, and subject to regulatory pressure that Matt thinks is often misdirected.

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Archegos and hidden leverage
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Bill Hwang lost $30B in two days. The mechanism: total return swaps that let him hold enormous positions banks didn't know about.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: Archegos One important thing that investment banks do is lend money to hedge funds to buy stocks. This is risky: If the stocks go down, the hedge fund might not repay the loans, and then the bank might lose money.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Archegos Was Too Busy for Margin Calls
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: The Archegos collapse is the definitive modern story of hidden leverage. Bill Hwang used total return swaps — derivatives that give you the economic exposure of owning a stock without actually owning it — to build enormous positions that didn't appear in public filings. His prime brokers each saw their own exposure but not the others'. When one stock moved against him, the forced unwind cascaded across the entire portfolio in two days. Banks lost billions. Matt covered it as a structural failure that was hiding in plain sight.

Hedge Funds / PE.title: Liquidity mismatch
Hedge Funds / PE.plain: Funds promise investors they can redeem — but hold illiquid assets. When redemptions spike, the mismatch breaks things.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote: ” Client redemptions brought assets under management to about $2.5 billion, “down from more than $6 billion a year ago” and $12 billion at its peak.
Hedge Funds / PE.quote_title: Losing Money Reduces the Risk of Too Much Money
Hedge Funds / PE.analysis: This is the fundamental tension in private fund investing that became acutely visible during the 2022-2023 rate rise. Funds promised investors quarterly or annual liquidity. The underlying assets — private credit loans, commercial real estate, private equity stakes — are inherently illiquid. When too many investors want to redeem simultaneously, funds impose gates or suspend redemptions entirely. Matt has covered this across several fund collapses as the return of a classic financial risk that should have been expected.

Regulation / SEC.title: How enforcement actually works
Regulation / SEC.plain: The SEC brings civil cases for money penalties; the DOJ brings criminal cases for prison. Both need to prove the same underlying facts.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Securities and Exchange Commission enforcement action against Under Armour Inc. for “pulling forward” revenue.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: Under Armour Sold Some Clothes Early
Regulation / SEC.analysis: The SEC and DOJ have different tools and different standards. The SEC brings civil cases and can impose fines, disgorgement, and industry bars. The DOJ brings criminal cases and can send people to prison — but needs to prove the same facts beyond a reasonable doubt rather than by a preponderance of evidence. The same conduct often generates parallel investigations. Matt covers the relationship between the agencies carefully: they sometimes coordinate, sometimes compete, and often settle cases at different times.

Regulation / SEC.title: The revolving door
Regulation / SEC.plain: Regulators leave for law firms and banks, then come back as regulators. Matt treats this as structural, not scandalous.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Regulators leave for law firms and banks, then come back as regulators. Matt treats this as structural, not scandalous. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Regulation / SEC.analysis: Matt doesn't moralize about this — he treats it as structural reality. Skilled securities lawyers move from the SEC to private practice, bring the knowledge of regulatory priorities and internal procedures with them, and return to the SEC later. The flow creates expertise in both directions and also creates incentives that are hard to fully untangle. The former SEC enforcement director who now defends the same types of cases they used to prosecute is a common figure in securities litigation.

Regulation / SEC.title: Regulation FD
Regulation / SEC.plain: Companies must disclose material information to everyone at once — no more quiet calls with favored analysts.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Companies must disclose material information to everyone at once — no more quiet calls with favored analysts. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Regulation / SEC.analysis: Before Reg FD, companies routinely held selective briefings with favored analysts — sharing quarterly guidance and strategic details before public announcements. The practice created information asymmetries that benefited large institutional investors over retail ones. Reg FD, adopted in 2000, requires simultaneous public disclosure. It's one of the few securities regulations Matt consistently credits as having clear, positive effects on market fairness.

Regulation / SEC.title: Gensler's SEC
Regulation / SEC.plain: The 2021–2025 SEC was notably aggressive — crypto, climate disclosure, payment for order flow. Most rules got litigated.
Regulation / SEC.quote: Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Gary Gensler said a full ban on payment for order flow is “on the table.” Robinhood fell about 3.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: Does Robinhood Need Payment for Order Flow?
Regulation / SEC.analysis: Gary Gensler's SEC was unusually ambitious — aggressive on crypto, climate disclosure, payment for order flow, stock buybacks. Most of the major rulemaking agenda got litigated. Courts struck down the climate disclosure rules, remanded the stock buyback rules, and limited the crypto enforcement agenda. Matt covered the rulemaking cycle as a collision between an expansive view of SEC authority and a judiciary increasingly skeptical of agency power.

Regulation / SEC.title: When courts push back
Regulation / SEC.plain: Courts increasingly strike down SEC rules as overreach — limiting what regulations can actually stick.
Regulation / SEC.quote: We have talked about this case a few times, including when it was filed and when the lower court struck down the tariffs, and it has always struck me as quite straightforward. The United States has a Constitution, and the Constitution gives Congress, not the president, the power to impose tariffs.
Regulation / SEC.analysis: The major questions doctrine — that agencies need explicit congressional authorization for economically significant regulations — has reshaped administrative law in ways that directly constrain the SEC. Courts have used it to strike down or limit rules on climate disclosure, gig worker classification, and vaccine mandates at other agencies. The SEC must now carefully calibrate its authority to survive judicial review, which means some rules that would be good policy are off the table.

Regulation / SEC.title: Payment for order flow
Regulation / SEC.plain: Robinhood gets paid by market makers to route orders to them. Is this a conflict of interest or just how markets work?
Regulation / SEC.quote: This is called “payment for order flow,” or PFOF. The intuition is that it is so profitable to trade with Robinhood customers that you can give the customers price improvement, and you can give Robinhood a check, and yet it is still a good business for you.
Regulation / SEC.quote_title: The SEC Wants More Stock Auctions
Regulation / SEC.analysis: Robinhood's business model depends on selling retail order flow to market makers who profit from executing those trades. The argument that this harms retail investors is intuitive but contested — market makers also compete for that flow and provide price improvement. Matt has covered this debate across multiple years and finds the economics genuinely complicated: 'free' trading may cost retail investors less than the commissions they used to pay, even if the routing creates conflicts.

Banking / Crises.title: Bank runs
Banking / Crises.plain: Banks borrow short (deposits) and lend long (loans). If depositors want their money back simultaneously, the math breaks.
Banking / Crises.quote: This problem is known as a “bank run” and is famous from, you know, the Diamond-Dybvig model that won a Nobel Prize last year, or from It’s a Wonderful Life . “The money’s not here.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: SVB Couldn’t Ignore Its Losses, But the Fed Can
Banking / Crises.analysis: The logic of a bank run is implacable: if everyone believes the bank will fail, everyone wants to withdraw, and that belief becomes self-fulfilling even if the bank was solvent to begin with. Deposit insurance was invented precisely to break this logic — if you know you'll get your money back regardless, you have no reason to run. But deposit insurance has limits, and for large uninsured depositors at SVB, the logic snapped back into force in 2023.

Banking / Crises.title: SVB's duration mismatch
Banking / Crises.plain: Silicon Valley Bank bought long-duration bonds when rates were low. When rates rose, the bonds lost value — and word got out.
Banking / Crises.quote: It is a bit harder to say exactly what SVB’s bad decision was. A simple answer is “it made a huge bet on interest rates staying low, which most prudent banks would not have done, and it blew up.
Banking / Crises.analysis: SVB's failure was a basic asset-liability mismatch that should have been visible in their filings. They had taken in short-term deposits (mostly from venture-backed startups flush with 2021 fundraising cash) and invested them in long-duration Treasury bonds when rates were near zero. When rates rose sharply, those bonds lost market value. When depositors needed their cash back, SVB couldn't sell the bonds without realizing enormous losses. The bank run took 48 hours once the losses became public.

Banking / Crises.title: Deposit insurance
Banking / Crises.plain: The $250K FDIC limit creates strange incentives — large depositors have reason to run faster than small ones.
Banking / Crises.quote: will contribute $5 billion of uninsured deposits each, while Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and Morgan Stanley will kick in $2.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: Big Banks Trust First Republic With Their Money
Banking / Crises.analysis: The $250K FDIC limit creates perverse incentives in a bank run. Large depositors — the ones most capable of moving quickly and with the most sophisticated information — have every reason to be first out the door. Small depositors, the ones the system is most designed to protect, tend to be slower and less informed. SVB's depositor base was almost entirely large uninsured accounts (startups and VCs), which meant that once confidence cracked, almost everyone had a reason to run.

Banking / Crises.title: Credit Suisse's AT1 wipeout
Banking / Crises.plain: Switzerland's forced UBS merger wiped out $17B in Credit Suisse AT1 bonds while equity got something — the opposite of normal bankruptcy order.
Banking / Crises.quote: Firms led by Credit Suisse Group AG lost more than $500 million on the secured bond portion alone, Bloomberg reported. I mean it’s not that good a trade.
Banking / Crises.quote_title: Buyout Loans Have a Bad Week
Banking / Crises.analysis: The Credit Suisse resolution showed that the rules can be rewritten in a crisis. AT1 bonds — hybrid debt that converts to equity or is written off in a stress scenario — had always been understood to sit above equity in the capital structure. When Swiss authorities engineered the UBS merger, they wiped out $17B of AT1 bonds while giving equity holders something. This inverted the expected priority order and sent shockwaves through the global AT1 market.

Banking / Crises.title: Too big to fail
Banking / Crises.plain: Large banks get implicit government backing — which creates moral hazard. The 2010 Dodd-Frank rules tried to fix this; the debate continues.
Banking / Crises.quote: That some fund run by the biggest exchange would examine each firm’s secret balance sheets and decide who is worthy of a bailout? What if … what if there was some sort of decentralized solution?
Banking / Crises.analysis: Dodd-Frank tried to solve TBTF through living wills (resolution plans) and enhanced capital requirements for systemically important financial institutions. Whether it succeeded is still contested. The 2023 bank failures required emergency backstops that looked a lot like the pre-Dodd-Frank bailouts the law was designed to prevent. Matt covers the persistent gap between regulatory design and practical outcomes.

Corporate Governance.title: Who actually controls a company
Corporate Governance.plain: The board is supposed to represent shareholders. In practice, management usually controls the board.
Corporate Governance.quote: The main perk of being the majority shareholder of a company is that you get to pick its board of directors. Zuckerberg is also, of course, the chief executive officer.
Corporate Governance.analysis: The formal answer is: shareholders elect a board, the board hires management, management runs the company. The real answer is: management usually controls the board. CEOs often have significant influence over director nominations, board members typically aren't chosen for their independence, and the corporate governance apparatus that's supposed to represent shareholders often represents management instead. Matt has documented this gap across hundreds of cases.

Corporate Governance.title: Dual-class shares
Corporate Governance.plain: Founders keep 10 votes per share, public shareholders get 1. Controversial but increasingly common in tech IPOs.
Corporate Governance.quote: Our high-vote stock will decrease from 20 votes per share to 10 votes per share. The voting power of our high-vote stock will sunset and automatically decrease to one vote per share in the event that Adam becomes permanently incapacitated or dies.
Corporate Governance.quote_title: We Is Sorry About the Weirdness
Corporate Governance.analysis: The tech industry has normalized a structure where founders keep 10x or more voting power per share, making hostile takeovers impossible and activist investors irrelevant. Google, Meta, Snap, and many others have used this structure. The argument for it: founders with long-term vision shouldn't be distracted by short-term shareholder pressure. The argument against: public investors are buying governance-free economic exposure with no ability to hold management accountable.

Corporate Governance.title: CEO pay
Corporate Governance.plain: Executive compensation is mostly stock — so CEOs are incentivized to care about the stock price above all else.
Corporate Governance.quote: Anything that shareholders have to vote on—director elections, executive compensation, etc.—Mark Zuckerberg can, and does, just decide by himself.
Corporate Governance.quote_title: Carl Icahn Wants an Oxydarko Vote
Corporate Governance.analysis: Executive compensation is genuinely hard to get right. You want CEOs to behave like owners, so you give them stock. But that also means they're incentivized to optimize for stock price, which isn't always the same as optimizing for the long-term business. You want performance conditions on the stock, but then you're paying consultants to design complex pay structures that CEOs optimize against. Matt has covered enough compensation committee disclosures to have developed a certain weary expertise.

Corporate Governance.title: ESG and shareholder proposals
Corporate Governance.plain: Investors file proposals on environmental and social issues. Companies fight them. The SEC decides which ones go to a vote.
Corporate Governance.quote: ESL ESG I wrote the other day about how JPMorgan Chase & Co. got in some hot water for agreeing to finance the European Super League, the now apparently dead proposal to start a lucrative new league for the biggest European soccer clubs.
Corporate Governance.analysis: Shareholder proposals under Rule 14a-8 give investors the ability to put governance and policy questions to a vote at annual meetings. Companies fight many of these proposals, asking the SEC to exclude them on various grounds. Environmental and social proposals went from fringe to mainstream to politically contested over a decade. Matt covers the procedural battles with genuine interest: the back-and-forth between companies, investors, and the SEC about which questions shareholders are entitled to ask.

Corporate Governance.title: Activist investors
Corporate Governance.plain: Hedge funds buy a stake and demand change — new directors, spinoffs, share buybacks. Sometimes they're right.
Corporate Governance.quote: Activist investors are supposed to use the more detailed Schedule 13D. But Musk checked the passive investor box.
Corporate Governance.analysis: The activist investment cycle follows a familiar pattern: fund acquires stake, files 13D, issues public letter demanding changes (new board seats, a sale, a spinoff, cost cuts), company responds, proxy fight or negotiated settlement, activist exits. Matt finds the mechanics interesting and the economics complicated — activists sometimes unlock real value and sometimes extract short-term gains at long-term cost. The difference is usually visible only in retrospect.

OpenAI / AI.title: The capped-profit structure
OpenAI / AI.plain: OpenAI was a nonprofit that created a 'capped-profit' subsidiary — investors could earn up to 100x but no more. Matt found this fascinating and slightly absurd.
OpenAI / AI.quote: All of OpenAI answers to its ultimate decision-making body, an independent nonprofit board of directors who do not own any equity in the OpenAI entities and who, broadly speaking, appoint themselves. They answer to their own consciences, not to any investors.
OpenAI / AI.analysis: OpenAI's governance was designed to make fundraising possible while maintaining a nonprofit mission. The capped-profit structure — investors could earn up to 100x return, after which profits flow to the nonprofit — was an elegant solution to a hard problem: how do you raise billions from investors who expect returns while remaining legally committed to humanity's benefit? Matt covered the structure with evident delight at its novelty and equally evident uncertainty about whether it would hold.

OpenAI / AI.title: The Altman firing and rehiring
OpenAI / AI.plain: OpenAI's board fired Sam Altman on a Friday, the company almost imploded over the weekend, he was back Monday. Corporate governance broke down in real time.
OpenAI / AI.quote: , and the nonprofit asserted itself dramatically last Friday when its board of directors fired its chief executive officer, Sam Altman, and threw everything into chaos. But for a moment ignore all of that and just think about OpenAI Inc.
OpenAI / AI.analysis: The November 2023 weekend was one of the most compressed corporate governance crises in history. The board fired Altman on Friday citing a loss of confidence, a majority of employees threatened to quit, investors threatened to sue, Microsoft stood ready to hire him, and by Monday he was reinstated with a new board. Matt covered it as a case study in how not to exercise board authority — the board had legitimate governance concerns but completely lacked the organizational support to act on them.

OpenAI / AI.title: AI and financial markets
OpenAI / AI.plain: Can AI systems trade better than humans? And what happens to markets when everyone's AI is trained on the same data?
OpenAI / AI.quote: “The Nonprofit’s principal beneficiary is humanity, not OpenAI investors,” explains OpenAI. In the second diagram, I have written the word “MONEY” in large green letters.
OpenAI / AI.analysis: The question of whether AI will transform financial markets is genuinely open. There are plausible cases where AI systems become the dominant traders — identifying patterns faster, processing more information, never sleeping. There are also plausible cases where widespread AI adoption homogenizes trading strategies in ways that increase systemic fragility. Matt covers the emerging cases — AI-advised index funds, algorithmic trading at new scales — without a strong prediction about where it ends.

OpenAI / AI.title: The GPU arms race
OpenAI / AI.plain: Building AI requires enormous capital for computing — creating an unprecedented financing challenge for young companies.
OpenAI / AI.quote: Maybe one day xAI will produce the only computer program that anyone uses, and will intermediate all of commerce, and will satisfy every human want, and will rule humanity with an iron fist. I mean!
OpenAI / AI.quote_title: Musk Has Money and xAI Wants Some
OpenAI / AI.analysis: Building competitive AI requires compute at a scale that's genuinely hard to finance. A single training run for a frontier model can cost hundreds of millions of dollars. The capital requirements have pushed AI companies toward unusual financing structures — OpenAI's capped-profit model, Anthropic's public benefit corporation, Mistral's European VC round. Matt has covered the financing challenge as an interesting corporate finance problem distinct from the technology.

OpenAI / AI.title: OpenAI going public
OpenAI / AI.plain: Converting from nonprofit to for-profit to enable an IPO — a corporate structure transition with no good precedent.
OpenAI / AI.quote: Converting from nonprofit to for-profit to enable an IPO — a corporate structure transition with no good precedent. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
OpenAI / AI.analysis: The conversion from nonprofit to for-profit is a governance transition with essentially no precedent. How do you value the public benefit mission? What happens to the capped-profit investors? Who represents the original nonprofit beneficiaries — humanity, apparently — in the new structure? Matt has written about this transformation with the same mixture of fascination and skepticism he brings to every novel corporate structure.

Fed / Central Banks.title: How interest rates work
Fed / Central Banks.plain: The Fed sets the federal funds rate — the rate banks charge each other overnight. Everything else flows from that.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: If you buy a five-year $100 bond that pays interest of 8%, and then interest rates go up by one percentage point, your bond is only worth $96. As rates go up, the yield of your bond has to go up, and to make the yield go up the price has to drop.
Fed / Central Banks.analysis: The fed funds rate is the overnight rate at which banks lend reserves to each other. It's the most important price in American finance because it anchors everything else — mortgage rates, corporate borrowing costs, the discount rate used to value every asset. When the Fed raises rates, it makes borrowing more expensive everywhere in the economy. When it cuts, it makes money cheaper. The mechanism is indirect but powerful.

Fed / Central Banks.title: Quantitative easing
Fed / Central Banks.plain: The Fed buys bonds to push money into the economy — and then has to sell them back when inflation rises.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: The firm’s balance sheet shows $200 worth of stuff. Somebody notices that the $200 worth of stuff is just a piece of paper with the words “this is worth $200” scrawled on it in crayon, and points that out online.
Fed / Central Banks.analysis: QE is the Fed buying assets (mostly Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities) with newly created money. The intended effect is to push down long-term interest rates and push investors into riskier assets. It worked, and it also inflated asset prices substantially. The unwinding — quantitative tightening — is always more awkward than the accumulation, because the Fed is selling bonds into a market that's pricing in the Fed's own behavior.

Fed / Central Banks.title: The yield curve
Fed / Central Banks.plain: Short rates above long rates signal a recession is coming. Except when it doesn't. Matt tracks this with measured skepticism.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: Short rates above long rates signal a recession is coming. Except when it doesn't. Matt tracks this with measured skepticism. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Fed / Central Banks.analysis: The yield curve inverted (short rates above long rates) before every recent US recession, creating a reliable-seeming predictor that Matt covers with measured skepticism. The mechanism is intuitive: if investors expect rates to fall in the future (because they expect a recession), they'll accept lower long-term yields now. But the yield curve has also inverted without a recession following, and the 2022 inversion lasted years — longer than any previous pre-recession inversion — before the data became ambiguous.

Fed / Central Banks.title: Fed independence
Fed / Central Banks.plain: Central bank independence from political pressure is a cornerstone of modern monetary policy — and increasingly contested.
Fed / Central Banks.quote: Central bank independence from political pressure is a cornerstone of modern monetary policy — and increasingly contested. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Fed / Central Banks.analysis: The Fed's political independence — the ability to make monetary policy decisions without direct executive control — is a product of institutional design and legal convention rather than constitutional mandate. Every administration since the Fed's creation has had tensions with it. The 2025 period of explicit political pressure on the Fed to cut rates, combined with discussions about firing the chair, tested the independence doctrine more directly than anything since the Nixon era.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: How an IPO is priced
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Banks run a roadshow, collect orders from institutions, set a price. The first-day pop measures how much money was left on the table.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: 4% between the beginning of Uber’s roadshow and the time it priced, but it was up about 36.5% between December 2015, when Uber first sold shares for $48.
IPOs / Capital Markets.analysis: The IPO process is a negotiation conducted through a roadshow — weeks of meetings between company management, their bankers, and institutional investors. Investors indicate interest at various prices, bankers build a book, and the final price is set at whatever clears the market (roughly). The first-day pop is always discussed as 'money left on the table' by the company, but issuers accept it as the cost of getting anchor investors excited about a deal.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: SPACs
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Blank-check companies that raise money first, find a target later. The 2020-21 SPAC boom ended badly for most retail investors.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: Like most SPACs, DWAC has a time limit to complete its deal: Its corporate charter says that, if it has not completed a merger by Sept. 8 — next Thursday — it has to close up shop and return its money to shareholders.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: The Trump SPAC Needs More Time
IPOs / Capital Markets.analysis: The SPAC boom of 2020-2021 created hundreds of blank-check companies that raised money on the promise of finding a target. The financial structure was remarkably favorable to SPAC sponsors — they got 20% of the company for minimal investment, effectively a giant warrant — and unfavorable to retail investors who held shares through dilutive mergers. Matt covered the mechanics carefully: the promote structure, the redemption rights, the incentives that produced a wave of low-quality deals.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: Direct listings
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Companies go public without issuing new shares or using underwriters — skipping the banker fees and the artificial first-day pop.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: One approach is the direct listing, where you just list your stock for trading and the price sort of comes to whatever it is. Then, if you like the price, you can do a stock offering at the actual market price a few weeks later, why not?
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: You’ll Never Want the Roadshow to End
IPOs / Capital Markets.analysis: Spotify and Coinbase pioneered a structure where companies go public without issuing new shares or paying underwriter commissions. Existing shareholders sell directly to the market; the company doesn't raise money in the listing itself. Matt found this interesting as a challenge to the traditional IPO model and genuinely useful for companies with strong brand recognition that didn't need the promotional machinery of a bank roadshow.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: Lockup expirations
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Insiders can't sell for 180 days after an IPO. When lockups expire, the overhang of insider selling hits the stock.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: You can go public without voting stock, without a lockup, without an IPO at all. The fact is that a lot of corporate founders are pretty suspicious of the method that Wall Street uses to price IPOs.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: You’ll Never Want the Roadshow to End
IPOs / Capital Markets.analysis: The 180-day lockup exists to prevent insiders from immediately dumping shares into a rising post-IPO market. When it expires, the overhang of potential insider selling creates predictable downward pressure on the stock. Matt has covered numerous cases where the lockup expiration was the beginning of a sustained decline — not because anything changed about the business, but because the supply of willing sellers suddenly increased.

IPOs / Capital Markets.title: The unicorn era
IPOs / Capital Markets.plain: Companies stayed private longer, raised at huge valuations, then went public into disappointment. Theranos was the extreme version.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote: One thing that I have said a few times around here is that, while an IPO is traditionally a big milestone in a company’s life, that is just less true of giant tech unicorns like Uber. (My colleague Shira Ovide has made similar points.
IPOs / Capital Markets.quote_title: Uber Is Already a Little Public
IPOs / Capital Markets.analysis: The 2015-2021 period produced dozens of companies valued at over a billion dollars as private companies, many of which went public at valuations that the public markets then marked down sharply. WeWork never got public. Uber and Lyft fell immediately. Peloton peaked and crashed. The common thread, in Matt's telling, was a mismatch between venture capital's narrative-driven valuation and public markets' earnings-driven valuation.

Legal / Courts.title: Delaware and corporate law
Legal / Courts.plain: Most US companies are incorporated in Delaware, making its Court of Chancery the most important corporate law court in the world.
Legal / Courts.quote: sued Elon Musk in Delaware to hold him to his agreement to buy Twitter for $54.20 per sh Programming note: Lol .
Legal / Courts.analysis: Most Fortune 500 companies are incorporated in Delaware not because they're headquartered there (almost none are) but because Delaware offers a sophisticated legal system, a large body of precedent, and a Court of Chancery that moves quickly. The judges are expert, the procedures are efficient, and the outcomes are predictable. Matt reads Chancery opinions the way some people read novels — for the reasoning, the characterizations of corporate misconduct, and the occasional dry wit.

Legal / Courts.title: How white-collar cases work
Legal / Courts.plain: The government needs a cooperating witness or documents. Wire fraud is the Swiss Army knife — almost anything can be charged as wire fraud.
Legal / Courts.quote: The government needs a cooperating witness or documents. Wire fraud is the Swiss Army knife — almost anything can be charged as wire fraud. (Matt returns to this theme across dozens of issues.)
Legal / Courts.analysis: The key to most white-collar prosecutions is documentary evidence and cooperating witnesses. Prosecutors offer plea deals to people lower in the organization in exchange for testimony about people higher up. They use wiretaps, emails, Slack messages, and financial records to reconstruct what happened. The wire fraud statute is the Swiss Army knife — almost any scheme that uses email or phones counts as wire fraud, which makes federal jurisdiction easy to establish.

Legal / Courts.title: The Sarbanes-Oxley legacy
Legal / Courts.plain: Post-Enron law that made CEOs personally certify financial statements. The threat of prison changed executive behavior.
Legal / Courts.quote: with its financial statements and other information. It’s basically as though the foreign company itself issued stock in the U.
Legal / Courts.analysis: SOX was passed within weeks of its introduction in 2002, driven by Enron and WorldCom. The requirement that CEOs and CFOs personally certify the accuracy of financial statements changed behavior: executives who previously might have been vaguely aware of accounting issues now had strong incentives to be certain. Whether this made financial statements more accurate or just moved responsibility around is debated, but it changed how companies manage their disclosure processes.

Legal / Courts.title: Spoofing and market manipulation
Legal / Courts.plain: Placing orders you intend to cancel to move the market, then trading against the movement. The line between strategy and crime is surprisingly thin.
Legal / Courts.quote: If these opportunistic strategies were, for instance, market manipulation, then presumably one of the regulators would have brought a case by now. But they haven’t.
Legal / Courts.analysis: Spoofing — placing orders you intend to cancel to create a false impression of supply or demand — was made explicitly illegal by Dodd-Frank in 2010. Before that, it existed in a legal gray area. The line between aggressive legitimate market-making and manipulation can be thin: every market maker cancels orders constantly, and the distinction between a legitimate cancellation and a spoofing order sometimes turns on intent, which is hard to prove.
